title: Trimming the Sails
subtitle: Hedging, diversification & derivatives

hook.metaphor: You can't stop the wind, but you can trim the sails, spread the load, and rig safety lines so a gust doesn't capsize you. Managing market risk is the same craft: you reduce your exposure to moves you don't want, while keeping the boat moving. The tools sound exotic — 'derivatives', 'hedging' — but the idea is just sensible seamanship.
hook.scenario: An exporter will be paid US$1 million in three months and is terrified the dollar will fall before then. A bank offers to lock in today's exchange rate for that future payment. Is taking that deal 'risky financial engineering' — or exactly the opposite?

learn: **Hedging** is taking an offsetting position so that if your exposure moves against you, the hedge moves *for* you, cancelling out the swing. Our exporter locking in today's exchange rate is a textbook hedge: whatever the dollar does, they now know exactly what they'll receive. Far from risky, it *removes* risk.

The main hedging tools are **derivatives** — contracts whose value derives from an underlying price. A **forward/future** locks in a price for a future date; an **option** is the right (not obligation) to trade at a set price; a **swap** exchanges one kind of payment for another (e.g. floating-rate for fixed). Used to hedge, they're seatbelts. Used to gamble, they're how people blow up — same tool, opposite intent.

**Diversification** is the other great lever: don't put all your exposure in one place, because uncorrelated risks partly cancel out ('don't put all your eggs in one basket'). A portfolio spread across many assets is steadier than one bet.

And the humble but vital tool: **risk limits** — hard caps on how much exposure a desk or trader may take. Limits ensure no single position can sink the firm, no matter how confident someone is (the rogue-trader lesson waits in Level 6).

The key judgement: hedging isn't free (it has a cost, and it also caps your *upside*). So firms decide how much risk to keep versus hedge based on their **risk appetite** — sometimes you accept some weather rather than pay to flatten every wave.
bridge: So at work, you can tell prudent risk management from reckless speculation by *intent*: the same derivative that hedges a real exposure (our exporter) can also be a naked gamble. Ask: *is this offsetting a risk we actually have, or creating a new bet?* And remember hedging costs something and caps upside — so how much to hedge is a deliberate choice against your risk appetite, not an automatic 'hedge everything'.

interactions.title: Lock in the rate?
interactions.prompt: The exporter locks in today's exchange rate for a future US$1m payment. Risky engineering, or sound management?

options.label: Reckless — derivatives are dangerous gambling.
options.feedback: Here the derivative *removes* risk rather than adding it. Locking a rate against a real, known future receipt is a classic hedge — the opposite of a gamble.

options.label: Sound hedging — it removes the uncertainty on a real exposure.
options.feedback: Exactly. They have a genuine FX exposure (US$1m due) and offset it. Whatever the dollar does, they know what they'll get. That's prudent seamanship.

options.label: Pointless — they should just hope the rate is fine.
options.feedback: Hoping is leaving the risk on. The whole point is to convert an uncertain future receipt into a known one — that certainty has real value to a business.

interactions.title: The toolkit
interactions.prompt: Match each risk-management tool to what it does.

pairs.right: An offsetting position that cancels a swing
Locks in a price for a future date
The right, not obligation, to trade at a set price
Exchanging one payment type for another (e.g. floating→fixed)
Spreading exposure so risks partly cancel out
A hard cap on how much exposure is allowed

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “You can't stop the wind but you can trim the sails — hedge real exposures with derivatives, diversify so risks cancel, and set hard limits — and the line between prudence and gambling is whether you're offsetting a risk or creating a new bet.”

options: Taking an offsetting position so an adverse move is cancelled out
checkpoint.explain: A hedge moves in your favour exactly when your exposure moves against you, neutralising the swing.

checkpoint.q: The same derivative can be prudent or reckless depending on…
options: Whether it offsets a real exposure or creates a new speculative bet
checkpoint.explain: Intent is everything: hedging a genuine exposure reduces risk; a naked position adds it.

checkpoint.q: Risk limits exist to ensure that…
options: No single position can grow large enough to sink the firm
checkpoint.explain: Hard caps contain exposure regardless of any individual's confidence — a key safeguard against blow-ups.
